policy_statement: Increase the proportion of vegetarian meals in schools and day care centres.
mechanism_description: Increasing the share of vegetarian meals in schools and day care centres shifts consumption away from emissions-intensive animal products, reducing food-related greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Dietary demand shift → ↓ livestock production & associated inputs → ↓ CH₄/N₂O + ↓ CO₂ (land-use/energy) → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Human activity/system changed: Public food procurement and consumption patterns in schools/day care shift toward vegetarian meals, reducing per-meal animal-product content.  
(2) Mediating process: Lower demand for livestock products reduces enteric fermentation methane, manure-related methane and nitrous oxide, feed production emissions (fertilizer N₂O, fuel use), and—depending on supply response—land conversion/land-use emissions.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in food-related lifecycle GHG emissions attributable to the served meals (and potentially broader market/behavior spillovers).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced food-system GHG emissions by lowering demand for emissions-intensive animal products (especially ruminant meat and dairy).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Changing diets/procurement reduces upstream agricultural and land-use-related GHG emissions; this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be resource-efficiency co-benefits (lower land/water intensity) and potential nature-based effects (reduced land pressure), the principal intended and largest climate effect across policies using this mechanism is reduced GHG emissions from food production.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the plant-based shift is implemented via food-waste reduction or composting, that would be a different mechanism; here the lever is substitution away from animal products. If plant-based options are highly processed and energy-intensive, mitigation benefits may be smaller but the dominant pathway remains emissions reduction relative to typical animal-based meals.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that increases plant-based/vegetarian meal share to displace animal products relies on the same core emissions-reduction pathway in the food system.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific mitigation subtypes (e.g., agricultural CH₄ reduction vs. land-use CO₂ vs. procurement/behavior) depend on policy design and evidence and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Benefits depend on what foods are displaced (largest for beef/lamb/dairy) and on supply-chain response; if substitution is mainly from low-emission animal products (e.g., poultry) to high-footprint plant products or if rebound increases consumption elsewhere, net reductions could be reduced, but the dominant intended mechanism remains mitigation via lower food-system GHGs.
key_indicators: “proportion of vegetarian meals will be increased”; “in schools and day care centres”
co_benefits: Potential public health/nutrition benefits; reduced local air pollution and water/land-use pressures from livestock supply chains.
instance_edge_case_notes: No explicit mandate wording (e.g., “shall/required”) or numeric target is provided, but the stated increase in the proportion of vegetarian meals in public settings functions as an institutional menu/procurement requirement.
